From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

Regional variation of British English spoken in South Asia

**South Asian English**, informally **Desi English**,[[1]](#cite_note-1) refers to [English dialects](/wiki/List_of_dialects_of_English "List of dialects of English") spoken in most modern-day [South Asian](/wiki/South_Asia "South Asia") countries, inherited from [British English](/wiki/British_English "British English") dialect. Also known as **Anglo-Indian English** during the [British Raj](/wiki/British_Raj "British Raj"), the English language was introduced to the [Indian subcontinent](/wiki/Indian_subcontinent "Indian subcontinent") in the early 17th century. Today it is spoken as a [second language](/wiki/Second_language "Second language") by about 350 million people, 20% of the total population.[[2]](#cite_note-2)

Although it is fairly homogeneous across the subcontinent, sharing "linguistic features and tendencies at virtually all linguistic levels", there are some differences based on various regional factors.[[3]](#cite_note-3)

South Asian English is sometimes just called "Indian English", as [British India](/wiki/British_India "British India") included most of modern-day [South Asia](/wiki/South_Asia "South Asia") (except [Afghanistan](/wiki/Afghanistan "Afghanistan")); but today, the varieties of English are officially divided according to the modern states:

* [Bangladeshi English](/wiki/Bangladeshi_English "Bangladeshi English")
* [Indian English](/wiki/Indian_English "Indian English")
* [Maldivian English](/wiki/Maldivian_English "Maldivian English")
* [Nepalese English](/wiki/Nepalese_English "Nepalese English")
* [Pakistani English](/wiki/Pakistani_English "Pakistani English")
* [Sri Lankan English](/wiki/Sri_Lankan_English "Sri Lankan English")

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=South_Asian_English&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

### British India

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=South_Asian_English&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: British India")]

See also: [Glossary of the British Raj](/wiki/Glossary_of_the_British_Raj "Glossary of the British Raj")

English-language public instruction began in the subcontinent in the 1830s during the [rule of the British East India Company](/wiki/Company_rule_in_India#Education "Company rule in India"). In 1835, English [replaced](/wiki/English_Education_Act_1835 "English Education Act 1835") [Persian](/wiki/Persian_language "Persian language") as the official language of the East India Company. [Lord Macaulay](/wiki/Thomas_Babington_Macaulay,_1st_Baron_Macaulay "Thomas Babington Macaulay, 1st Baron Macaulay") played a major role in introducing English and Western concepts into educational institutions in British-India. He supported the replacement of Persian by English as the official language, the use of English as the medium of instruction in all schools, and the training of English-speaking Indians as teachers.[[4]](#cite_note-Indian_English_John_MacKenzie_2013-4) Throughout the 1840s and 1850s, primary, middle, and high schools were opened in many districts of [British India](/wiki/Presidencies_and_provinces_of_British_India "Presidencies and provinces of British India"), with most high schools offering English language instruction in some subjects. In 1857, just before the end of East India Company rule, universities that were modeled on the [University of London](/wiki/University_of_London "University of London") and used English as the medium of instruction were established in Bombay, Calcutta and Madras. During the [British Raj](/wiki/British_Raj "British Raj") (1858 to 1947), English-language penetration increased throughout the subcontinent. This was driven in part by the gradually increasing hiring of Indians in the [civil services](/wiki/Civil_services "Civil services"). At the time of [Indian independence](/wiki/Indian_Independence_Act_1947 "Indian Independence Act 1947") in 1947, English was the only functional *[lingua franca](/wiki/Lingua_franca "Lingua franca")* in the region.

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=South_Asian_English&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: See also")]

* [Commonwealth English](/wiki/Commonwealth_English "Commonwealth English")
